title: The Second Coming of Steve Jobs by Alan Deutschman
description: Focuses intensively on Jobs' wilderness years (1985-1997) and his reasoning during failure and comeback. Documents his thought processes during NeXT development and Pixar growth, showing how he processed setbacks and recalibrated his approach.

title: All Things Digital Conference Interviews (2003, 2004, 2005, 2007, 2010)
description: Unscripted Q&A sessions where Jobs explains his reasoning about strategic decisions, competitive positioning, and product philosophy. Shows real-time thinking about industry evolution and Apple's positioning.

title: The Pixar Touch by David Price
description: Documents Jobs' reasoning during Pixar's development, showing how he applied lessons from Apple failures to building a different kind of creative organization. Reveals his thinking about creative processes and business model innovation.

title: Return to the Little Kingdom by Michael Moritz
description: Early Apple history by someone who observed Jobs closely during formative decisions. Documents the reasoning behind key early choices that established his decision-making patterns and core beliefs about technology and users.

title: The Perfect Thing by Steven Levy
description: Deep dive into iPod development decisions, including documented reasoning about user experience tradeoffs, business model choices, and market timing. Shows Jobs' decision-making process for his most successful product launch.

title: Wired Magazine Profile 1996 - 'The Next Insanely Great Thing'
description: Interview during Jobs' lowest point professionally, revealing his reasoning about failure, learning, and what he was reconceptualizing about technology and markets. Shows cognitive recalibration in real-time.

title: Fortune Magazine Interviews (1998-2008)
description: Series of interviews during Apple's resurgence where Jobs explains his reasoning about strategic pivots, organizational changes, and product priorities. Shows evolution of his thinking about scalable innovation.

title: 1985 Playboy Interview
description: Extensive interview revealing Jobs' thinking about technology, society, and business strategy during Apple's early success. Shows his reasoning about market positioning, user experience priorities, and competitive strategy before his major failures taught him humility.

title: Stanford Commencement Address 2005
description: Jobs' most revealing public exposition of his core decision-making philosophy, including 'connecting the dots,' following passion, and confronting mortality. Shows his meta-cognitive framework for major life decisions.

title: Macworld Keynote Addresses (1984, 1998-2011)
description: Jobs' own explanations of product decisions and strategic reasoning delivered to core audiences. Shows how he framed problems and solutions, revealing his mental models for innovation and market positioning.

title: Steve Jobs by Walter Isaacson
description: Based on extensive interviews with Jobs before his death, this biography captures his reasoning processes through direct quotes and documented decision-making moments. Isaacson had unique access to Jobs' thinking during critical periods including the return to Apple and development of revolutionary products.

title: Young Steve Jobs by Schlender & Gassée
description: Written by journalists who covered Jobs from the early Apple days, contains firsthand accounts of his decision-making evolution from the 1980s through early 2000s. Includes direct observations of his reasoning patterns during product development cycles.

title: The Third Apple by Wozniak & Smith
description: Co-founder Wozniak's account provides unique insight into Jobs' early decision-making patterns, value conflicts between technical excellence and market appeal, and his evolution from technical tinkerer to business strategist.

title: Inside Steve's Brain by Leander Kahney
description: Systematic analysis of Jobs' decision-making patterns across different contexts - hiring, firing, product development, marketing. Identifies consistent cognitive frameworks and reasoning heuristics he employed.

title: What Would Steve Jobs Do? by Peter Sander
description: Systematic extraction of Jobs' decision-making principles based on documented choices across multiple contexts. Attempts to codify his reasoning frameworks and decision heuristics into replicable patterns.
